message.content:
## Synthesis: research report

**Question:** BEST LOCAL / OPEN-SOURCE TEXT-TO-SPEECH (TTS) MODELS TO REPLACE OR AUGMENT KOKORO for an English educational-podcast pipeline, as of 2026. Baseline to beat: Kokoro-82M (hexgrad/Kokoro-82M), currently run locally on Apple Silicon (CPU + MPS), producing 24kHz audio, multiple voices (af_heart/am_onyx/bf_isabella/bm_george etc.), American + British English, two-voice format (narrator + question voice). Evaluate candidates across these angles: (1) QUALITY/NATURALNESS — which open/local TTS models beat Kokoro on naturalness and intelligibility for English (candidates to assess: XTTS-v2/Coqui, Piper, StyleTTS2, F5-TTS, Fish-Speech/OpenAudio, Parler-TTS, MeloTTS, Orpheus-TTS, Chatterbox, Kitten-TTS, Zonos, and any newer 2025-2026 open models) — report MOS/quality benchmarks, license (commercial/redistribution OK?), and model size. (2) WORD/PHONEME-LEVEL TIMING & ALIGNMENT — which models natively output word/phoneme timestamps (durations), and what forced-alignment tools (Montreal Forced Aligner, WhisperX, aeneas, ctc-forced-aligner) best produce a text↔audio timing map from existing audio — this enables content-aware speed compression, script-highlighting, and a true text-to-audio map. (3) HIGH-SPEED COMPREHENSIBILITY — which model's output stays most intelligible under aggressive time-compression / at high playback speed (crisp consonants, clean formant transitions); also the best FORMANT/robotic option (eSpeak-NG, RHVoice, DECtalk-like) for an extreme 16x 'ultra' track. (4) MULTI-VOICE & VOICE CONTROL — voice variety, voice cloning, prosody/rate control, and ease of adding new voices for a narrator+question two-speaker format. (5) LOCAL-INFERENCE PRACTICALITY on Apple Silicon and CPU — real-time factor / batch render speed, RAM/VRAM needs, MPS/CoreML/ONNX support, latency, and integration effort to swap into a Python pipeline that today calls Kokoro. For EACH recommended model give: quality vs Kokoro, license, local-inference feasibility on a Mac, whether it emits alignment, and a clear verdict on whether it's worth switching to (or adding alongside Kokoro). Prefer authoritative/benchmark sources and recent (2025-2026) comparisons; flag where claims are vendor marketing vs independent evaluation.

23 claims survived 3-vote adversarial verification. Merge semantic duplicates and synthesize.

## Confirmed claims
### [0] TTS Arena ranks text-to-speech models via blind pairwise human preference voting (users hear two synthesized samples and pick the more natural one, with model identities hidden until after voting), rather than via traditional MOS scoring.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://huggingface.co/blog/arena-tts (primary)
Quote: "After listening to each sample, the user will vote on which model sounds more natural ... [model names are] revealed only after a vote is submitted"
Verifier evidence (high): Claim is fully supported by the primary source. The HF blog (huggingface/blog/arena-tts.md) states verbatim: "After listening to each sample, the user will vote on which model sounds more natural" and "Due to the risks of human bias and abuse, model names will be revealed only after a vote is submitted." This directly confirms blind pairwise preference voting with identities hidden until after voting. The claim's contrast with MOS is also accurate: the source explicitly notes objective metrics (WER) are "unreliable" and subjective MOS studies are "typically small-scale," and instead ranks models via "an algorithm similar to the Elo rating system" (implemented via Bradley-Terry) over aggregated pairwise votes. Corroborated independently by the official TTS Arena V2 'About' page and third-party descriptions (tts.ai). No contradicting evidence found. Source is authoritative (the team running the arena, TTS-AGI) and current (V2 active 2025-2026, vote-threshold update Oct 2025). Not marketing — it is a methodology description.

### [1] TTS Arena aggregates the blind preference votes into a leaderboard using an Elo-style rating algorithm, the same family of ranking used in chess, rather than absolute quality scores.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://huggingface.co/blog/arena-tts (primary)
Quote: "ranked using an algorithm similar to the Elo rating system, commonly used in chess and other games"
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is directly supported verbatim by the primary source. The Hugging Face blog (https://huggingface.co/blog/arena-tts) states: "Models will be ranked using an algorithm similar to the Elo rating system, commonly used in chess and other games." This matches the claim's assertion of an Elo-style rating from the chess family. The blog further confirms blind voting ("model names are revealed only after a vote is submitted") and that votes raise/lower ratings relatively — a comparative, not absolute, scoring scheme; the blog explicitly motivates this by noting objective metrics (WER) are unreliable and MOS experiments are small-scale. Independent corroboration: the TTS Arena V2 about/leaderboard pages reference an "arena (ELO) score," and third-party writeups (Artificial Analysis, MarkTechPost) describe the same Elo-based blind-comparison methodology. Source quality is appropriate (official primary source from TTS-AGI creators). The paraphrase "Elo-style / same family used in chess" is accurate, not an overreach. No contradicting evidence found.

### [2] ctc-forced-aligner performs text-to-audio forced alignment using Hugging Face CTC models (Wav2Vec2/HuBERT/MMS) and outputs segment-level timestamps as JSON with start, end, and text fields, enabling a text-to-audio timing map from existing audio.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://github.com/MahmoudAshraf97/ctc-forced-aligner (primary)
Quote: "Produces JSON with aligned segments containing: "start", "end", and "text" fields. Example shows word-level alignment with millisecond precision."
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is fully and directly supported by the primary source. The ctc-forced-aligner README (github.com/MahmoudAshraf97/ctc-forced-aligner) explicitly states it "leverages the power of Wav2Vec2, HuBERT, and MMS models for accurate alignment" and performs "forced alignment between text and audio." The README's literal output example confirms the exact JSON structure claimed: {"text": "...", "segments": [{"start": 0.000, "end": 1.234, "text": "This"}, ...]} — i.e. segments each containing "start", "end", and "text" fields. The default model is MahmoudAshraf/mms-300m-1130-forced-aligner (a Hugging Face CTC model). Corroborated independently by PyPI (pypi.org/project/ctc-forced-aligner), the HuggingFace model card, and the repo source (alignment_utils.py). The tool additionally exposes generate_srt()/generate_webvtt() and configurable granularity (sentence/word/character via split_size), which strengthens rather than contradicts the claim. The repo is actively maintained (recent perf/dependency improvements), so the claim is current. Minor nuance: the claim says "segment-level" while the tool supports word/char/sentence granularity, but "segment" is the README's own term for the timestamped unit, so this is accurate, not an overreach. No contradicting source found.

### [3] Montreal Forced Aligner (MFA), a GMM-HMM/Kaldi-based traditional aligner, outperforms modern ASR-based aligners WhisperX and MMS (wav2vec2) on English word-level forced alignment accuracy.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://arxiv.org/html/2406.19363v1 (primary)
Quote: "MFA outperformed both WhisperX and MMS, revealing a shortcoming of modern ASR systems."
Verifier evidence (high): CLAIM CONFIRMED (not refuted). The primary source is the peer-reviewed paper "Tradition or Innovation: A Comparison of Modern ASR Methods for Forced Alignment" (arXiv 2406.19363), published at Interspeech 2024 (ISCA Archive rousso24_interspeech, Technion) — a legitimate peer-reviewed venue, not marketing. The supporting quote accurately reflects the conclusion: MFA outperforms WhisperX and MMS at ALL tolerance thresholds for English word-level forced alignment. Quantitatively, on TIMIT MFA hits 41.6% of word boundaries within ±10ms vs WhisperX 22.4% and MMS 18.6%; on Buckeye MFA 39.8% vs WhisperX 18.8% / MMS 25.0%. Authors' own conclusion: "the classical GMM-HMM architecture still outperforms simple adaptations of the modern end-to-end ASR systems for forced alignment." Independent practitioner corroboration: WhisperX GitHub issue #1247 (Oct 2025) reports WhisperX word timestamps "significantly off" vs MFA validated in Praat. Targeted searches for the opposite/contradicting result returned only confirmations. KEY CAVEAT (qualifies scope, does not refute): the comparison only counts words correctly recognized by WhisperX/MMS, on English read/conversational speech (TIMIT/Buckeye), and uses ASR systems not purpose-built for FA — but this scoping is acknowledged by the authors and is fully consistent with the claim as worded ("English word-level forced alignment accuracy"). Claim is current (2024 paper + 2025 confirmation) and source quality matches claim strength.

### [4] On TIMIT word-level alignment at a 50ms tolerance, MFA achieves 89.4% accuracy versus 82.4% for WhisperX and 75.7% for MMS.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://arxiv.org/html/2406.19363v1 (primary)
Quote: "On TIMIT word-level alignment at the 50ms threshold: - MFA: 89.4% accuracy - WhisperX: 82.4% accuracy - MMS: 75.7% accuracy"
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is fully supported by the primary source (arXiv 2406.19363, Rousso et al., "Tradition or Innovation: A Comparison of Modern ASR Methods for Forced Alignment," peer-reviewed Interspeech 2024). WebFetch of the arXiv HTML returned Table 1 verbatim: MFA 89.4%, WhisperX 82.4%, MMS 75.7% word-level accuracy on TIMIT at the t≤50ms threshold — matching the claim's numbers exactly (including the correct ordering MFA > WhisperX > MMS). An independent WebSearch corroborated the same figures and paper, and confirmed the paper's conclusion that "MFA outperforms MMS and WhisperX timestamps at all thresholds" (so the 50ms figure is not cherry-picked). Source quality is high: a peer-reviewed academic benchmark on the standard TIMIT dataset, not vendor marketing. A separate GitHub WhisperX issue (#1247) independently confirms the directional finding that WhisperX word timestamps are less accurate than MFA. No contradicting evidence found. The claim is correctly scoped to TIMIT (the paper also tests Buckeye, where numbers differ, but the claim does not overreach to that). Recent enough (2024) for a 2026 query.

### [5] mlx-audio is an MIT-licensed library that runs multiple TTS models (including Kokoro) locally on Apple Silicon using Apple's MLX framework, optimized for M-series chips.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://github.com/Blaizzy/mlx-audio (primary)
Quote: "Fast inference optimized for Apple Silicon (M series chips)"
Verifier evidence (high): All four assertions in the claim are confirmed by primary sources. (1) LICENSE: directly verified at github.com/Blaizzy/mlx-audio/blob/main/LICENSE — "MIT License, Copyright (c) 2024 Prince Canuma" (Prince Canuma is the GitHub user "Blaizzy"). (2) Multiple TTS models incl. Kokoro: README/PyPI/docs list Kokoro explicitly ("Fast, high-quality multilingual TTS") alongside Qwen3-TTS, CSM, Dia, Voxtral, KittenTTS, etc. (3) Built on Apple's MLX framework: verbatim repo description — "a TTS, STT and STS library built on Apple's MLX framework, providing efficient speech analysis on Apple Silicon." (4) Optimized for M-series: the exact supporting quote "Fast inference optimized for Apple Silicon (M series chips)" plus "native M1/M2/M3/M4 acceleration" from PyPI. The quote fully supports the Apple-Silicon-optimization portion; the license and model-support portions are independently corroborated by the LICENSE file and README/PyPI. The claim is descriptive (what the tool is and targets), not an extraordinary benchmark claim, so a primary source is sufficient. No contradicting evidence found across GitHub, PyPI, project docs, or mirrors. Only minor caveat: "optimized for" originates from the author's self-description, but since MLX is Apple's framework purpose-built for Apple Silicon, "built on MLX" inherently entails Apple-Silicon targeting — the claim does not overreach into measured-superiority territory.

### [6] mlx-audio supports Kokoro natively with 54 voice presets, meaning the existing Kokoro pipeline could be re-hosted on MLX rather than CPU/MPS.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://github.com/Blaizzy/mlx-audio (primary)
Quote: "Kokoro: "Fast, high-quality multilingual TTS" with 54 voice presets"
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is well-supported by the primary source and independently corroborated. The official maintainer's repo (github.com/Blaizzy/mlx-audio) and PyPI (pypi.org/project/mlx-audio) both state verbatim: "Kokoro is a fast, multilingual TTS model with 54 voice presets" — matching the claim's "54 voice presets" exactly. mlx-audio is purpose-built on Apple's MLX framework for Apple Silicon, and Kokoro is loaded via MLX community quantizations: model = load_model("mlx-community/Kokoro-82M-bf16") (also 8bit and 4bit variants). The documented voice set includes the user's exact pipeline voices (af_heart, bm_george, bf_alice, am_adam, etc.), so the inference that "the existing Kokoro pipeline could be re-hosted on MLX rather than CPU/MPS" is directly enabled, not an overreach. Corroborating independent sources: dedicated gabrimatic/kokoro-mlx project ("Kokoro-82M text-to-speech inference on Apple Silicon via MLX"), Pro-official/mlx-audio fork, and the blaizzy.github.io/mlx-audio docs site. No contradicting evidence found. Source is authoritative (canonical library maintainer), current (active PyPI package, 2026), and not marketing fluff — the 54-preset/Kokoro-support facts are library capabilities, not benchmark cherry-picks. The only marketing-flavored item (a third-party blog's "40% faster" figure) is not relied upon by this claim.

### [7] mlx-audio includes forced-alignment/timestamp capabilities (Qwen3-ForcedAligner for word-level audio alignment with start/end times, Parakeet word-level timestamps), enabling a text-to-audio timing map.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://github.com/Blaizzy/mlx-audio (primary)
Quote: "Qwen3-ForcedAligner provides "Word-level audio alignment" with start/end times. Parakeet offers "word-level timestamps.""
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is well-supported by primary and corroborating sources. (1) Qwen3-ForcedAligner: mlx-audio supports loading mlx-community/Qwen3-ForcedAligner-0.6B-8bit, with example code emitting per-item word timing: `for item in result: print(f"[{item.start_time:.2f}s - {item.end_time:.2f}s] {item.text}")`. The OFFICIAL Qwen HuggingFace card (huggingface.co/Qwen/Qwen3-ForcedAligner-0.6B) confirms it can "align text–speech pairs and return word or character level timestamps" and "supports timestamp prediction for arbitrary units within up to 5 minutes of speech in 11 languages" — so this is a primary-source-backed, real capability, not marketing. Independent reports cite ~6ms timing MAE and 100% text-match vs official backend. (2) Parakeet: the mlx-audio docs (blaizzy.github.io/mlx-audio/models/stt/parakeet/) have a section titled "Sentence and Word Timestamps" returning AlignedSentence/AlignedToken objects with start/end/duration/tokens; the repo description advertises STT "with word-level timestamps." (3) Both are 2025-2026 current models (Qwen3, Parakeet v3), and mlx-audio (Blaizzy/Prince Canuma) is an actively maintained, well-known Apple Silicon MLX library on PyPI/GitHub. Together these natively produce a text↔audio timing map on a Mac. Minor caveat (not refuting): visible Parakeet examples emphasize sentence-level iteration, with word-level granularity via AlignedToken; and "word-level timestamp" precision for Parakeet is token-based, but the core claim — both tools emit start/end alignment enabling a timing map — holds.

### [8] Mach1 nonuniform time-scale modification keeps speech comprehensible at much higher rates than real time, up to 2.56-4.15x real time (390-673 words per minute).
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.mangolassi.org/covell/1997-061/index.html (primary)
Quote: "The resulting compressed speech remains comprehensible at much higher rates: as high as 2.56 to 4.15 times real time and 390 to 673 wpm."
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is directly and accurately supported by the primary source. The mangolassi.org page (author-hosted copy of Covell, Withgott & Slaney, "Mach1 for Nonuniform Time-Scale Modification of Speech," Interval Research Corp, published at IEEE ICASSP 1998, IEEE Xplore doc 674439) states verbatim: "The resulting compressed speech remains comprehensible at much higher rates: as high as 2.56 to 4.15 times real time and 390 to 673 wpm." An INDEPENDENT mirror on Purdue's engineering domain (co-author Malcolm Slaney's site, engineering.purdue.edu/~malcolm/interval/1997-061/writeup.html) corroborates: "For rates between 2.5 and 4.2 times real time, there was no significant comprehension loss with increasing Mach1 compression rates" and "The resulting speaking rate ranged from 390 to 673 wpm (mean=500...)." This is a primary, peer-reviewed academic source — not vendor marketing, press release, or forum speculation. No credible source contradicts it; rather, Google's "Speedy" algorithm is a modern reimplementation built on this work, indicating it remains a respected foundational result. The 1997/1998 date is not disqualifying: this is a foundational psychoacoustics result on time-compressed-speech intelligibility (a stable human-perception finding), not a fast-moving ML benchmark. Minor nuance: the claim attributes the figures to Mach1 nonuniform compression specifically, which the source supports; the wpm range (390-673) is identical across both author-hosted copies.

### [9] At identical overall compression rates, listener comprehension of nonuniformly (Mach1) compressed speech is 5 to 31 percentage points higher than linearly/uniformly compressed speech, with 15% faster response times.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.mangolassi.org/covell/1997-061/index.html (primary)
Quote: "At identical overall compression rates, listener comprehension for Mach1-compressed speech increased between 5 and 31 percentage points over that for linearly compressed speech, and response times dropped by 15%."
Verifier evidence (high): Claim is verbatim-supported by a peer-reviewed primary source. I extracted the exact text from the published IEEE paper (Covell, Withgott & Slaney, "Mach1: Nonuniform Time-Scale Modification of Speech," ICASSP 1998; IRC TR 1997-061, ee.columbia.edu/~dpwe/papers/CovWS98-mach1.pdf): "At identical overall compression rates, listener comprehension for Mach1-compressed speech increased between 5 and 31 percentage points over that for linearly compressed speech, and response times dropped by 15%." These are the paper's own controlled experimental results: a 14-subject within-subjects listener test, compression 2.56-4.15x real time, balanced pool-A/pool-B design with response times measured per question. The claim's wording is an accurate paraphrase, not an overreach. Caveats (qualify, do not refute): (a) single study, n=14, conducted by the technology's inventors at Interval Research, not independently replicated for this exact figure; (b) the 5-31 range is content-dependent extremes per the PDF (short dialogs ~23 avg/up to 31 points, monologs ~10, long dialogs ~5), so it is not a uniform effect; (c) later literature (e.g. arxiv 1901.07239, ScienceDirect S0167639303001092) questions whether nonlinear benefit is largely attributable to silence/pause removal rather than prosody modeling, but this critiques mechanism, not the reported numbers. No credible source contradicts the figures. Age (1998) is fine for a foundational psychoacoustics result.

### [10] Between 2.5x and 4.2x real time, nonuniform (Mach1) compression yields about 17 percentage points better comprehension than linear compression, and Mach1 showed no significant comprehension loss as its compression rate increased across that range.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.mangolassi.org/covell/1997-061/index.html (primary)
Quote: "At compression rates between 2.5 and 4.2 times real time, comprehension of Mach1-compressed speech is 17 percentage points better than that of linearly compressed speech."
Verifier evidence (high): The primary source (Covell, Withgott & Slaney, Mach1 paper, IEEE ICASSP 1998, mirrored at mangolassi.org/covell/1997-061 and engineering.purdue.edu/~malcolm/interval/1997-061) supports BOTH halves of the claim nearly verbatim: (a) "At compression rates between 2.5 and 4.2 times real time, comprehension of Mach1-compressed speech is 17 percentage points better than that of linearly compressed speech," and (b) "For rates between 2.5 and 4.2 times real time, there was no significant comprehension loss with increasing Mach1 compression rates." No overreach or misread; the 17pp figure and the no-loss-across-2.5-to-4.2x finding are both directly stated. The source is a legitimate peer-reviewed conference paper (not marketing/forum/press release), so source quality matches the claim's specificity. The claim is old (1997-1998) and a broader 'non-uniform always wins' narrative IS contested by later independent work (2014 JASA, PubMed 24606289; ScienceDirect S0167639315000977 found a uniform-algorithm advantage in speech-in-noise; word intelligibility of heavily compressed speech not improved by natural temporal patterns). HOWEVER, those contradictions target out-of-scope conditions (speech-in-noise, word-level intelligibility, the fast 1.4x rate). Independent follow-up literature actually CORROBORATES this claim's specific scope: "At ultra fast speaking rates (2.5 and 4.1) MACH1 improves comprehension and is preferable to linearly compressed speech." Since the claim is precisely scoped to comprehension at 2.5x-4.2x, it is accurately supported by the quote and survives the contradicting evidence (which applies to different conditions).

### [11] DECtalk is available as a modern, locally-buildable formant text-to-speech synthesizer that compiles for macOS on both Apple Silicon and Intel (as well as Linux x86_64/aarch64, Windows, and WebAssembly), making it feasible as the robotic/formant track in a local Mac-based podcast pipeline.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://github.com/dectalk/dectalk (primary)
Quote: "Modern builds for the 90s/00s DECtalk text-to-speech application ... macOS/iOS (Apple Silicon and Intel) ... Try it out in your web browser with WebAssembly"
Verifier evidence (high): The primary source (github.com/dectalk/dectalk, develop branch README) explicitly states it provides "Working Linux/Windows binaries (i386, x86_64 and aarch64), MacOS/iOS (Apple Silicon and Intel)" and "Try it out in your web browser with WebAssembly" (webspeak demo). It is locally buildable on macOS via documented steps: install Xcode CLI tools (xcode-select --install), then in src/: autoreconf -si && ./configure && make -j (output in /dist). The claim's platform list (macOS Apple Silicon + Intel, Linux x86_64/aarch64, Windows, WebAssembly) is a faithful paraphrase of the source, not an overreach. INDEPENDENT corroboration found: (a) a MacPorts port exists (ports.macports.org/port/dectalk, install via 'sudo port install dectalk', upstream = same GitHub repo) confirming macOS packaging independently of the project's own claims; (b) echogarden-project/dectalk-wasm independently confirms the WebAssembly port; (c) dectalk.de/webspeak hosts a working web demo. DECtalk is genuinely a Klatt-based formant synthesizer (the original DEC "voice of Stephen Hawking"), matching the claim's robotic/formant-track framing. No credible source disputes macOS/Apple Silicon buildability. The only minor caveat surfaced is that prebuilt macOS binaries are harder to find than Linux/Windows ones, but the claim asserts buildability (which is documented and MacPorts-confirmed), not prebuilt availability. Claim is non-extraordinary, current, well-supported by primary + independent sources.

### [12] The DECtalk source code carries a proprietary, restrictive FONIX Corporation license rather than a permissive open-source license, meaning redistribution and commercial use are not freely permitted without written authorization — a licensing red flag for a podcast pipeline intending redistribution.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://github.com/dectalk/dectalk (primary)
Quote: "This software is proprietary to and embodies the confidential technology of FONIX Corporation ... Possession, use, or copying of this software and media is authorized only pursuant to a valid written license from FONIX or an authorized sublicensor."
Verifier evidence (high): The supporting quote is verbatim-confirmed from the primary source, the LICENCE file at github.com/dectalk/dectalk (raw at raw.githubusercontent.com/dectalk/dectalk/develop/LICENCE). It states: "This software is proprietary to and embodies the confidential technology of FONIX Corporation" and "Possession, use, or copying of this software and media is authorized only pursuant to a valid written license from FONIX or an authorized sublicensor," plus "The name of FONIX Corporation may not be used to endorse or promote products derived from this software without specific prior written permission." This is a binding license, not marketing. Attempts to refute failed: while informal forums (EltenLink, bluegrasspals DECtalk list) loosely call DECtalk "open source" because source is publicly available, they explicitly confirm it is NOT under a permissive license (not BSD/MIT) and that real legal/redistribution restrictions persist (e.g. cannot be included in Linux distros; "someone, somewhere, has licensing rights"). A sibling fork (RetroBunn/dt51) warns "This repository is not under any open-source license. Use at your own risk." IP-ownership history (Force Computers -> Fonix/SpeechFX, both now defunct ~2014/2020) makes a permissive relicense unlikely and leaves the restrictive 2002-2003 terms in force. All independent evidence corroborates rather than contradicts the claim's core assertion: proprietary FONIX license, written authorization required, redistribution/commercial use not freely permitted = genuine licensing red flag for a redistribution pipeline.

### [13] eSpeak NG is a formant-synthesis speech engine whose output is clear and usable at high speeds, though less natural than recording-based (concatenative/neural) synthesizers — making it suited as the crisp 'robotic' formant option for an extreme high-speed track.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://github.com/espeak-ng/espeak-ng (primary)
Quote: "The speech is clear, and can be used at high speeds, but is not as natural or smooth as larger synthesizers which are based on human speech recordings."
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is supported near-verbatim by the primary source. The espeak-ng README (github.com/espeak-ng/espeak-ng) states: "eSpeak NG uses a 'formant synthesis' method. This allows many languages to be provided in a small size. The speech is clear, and can be used at high speeds, but is not as natural or smooth as larger synthesizers which are based on human speech recordings." The claim restates this accurately on every axis: (a) formant-synthesis engine, (b) clear/usable at high speeds, (c) less natural than recording-based (concatenative/neural) synthesizers. Independent corroboration: Wikipedia (en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ESpeak) confirms it "uses a formant synthesis method," is shipped in NVDA and other screen readers (a use case where high WPM playback is standard for blind users), and notes Google Translate later replaced many eSpeak-predecessor voices with proprietary ones — consistent with eSpeak prioritizing intelligibility over naturalness. The only added element — "suited as the crisp robotic formant option for an extreme high-speed track" — is a reasonable application of the cited properties, not a factual overreach or a vendor/marketing claim (the source language is self-deprecating, not promotional). The claim is current: eSpeak NG is actively maintained and its formant architecture is stable, so the fast-moving-field staleness concern does not apply.

### [14] eSpeak NG can convert text into phoneme codes and be adapted as a phonemizer/front end for another speech synthesis engine, which is exactly how it serves as the phoneme backbone for neural TTS systems like Kokoro.
Vote: 2-1 · Source: https://github.com/espeak-ng/espeak-ng (primary)
Quote: "Can translate text into phoneme codes, so it could be adapted as a front end for another speech synthesis engine"
Verifier evidence (high): Part 1 of the claim is verbatim from the primary source. The official espeak-ng GitHub repo (https://github.com/espeak-ng/espeak-ng) Features section states: "Can translate text into phoneme codes, so it could be adapted as a front end for another speech synthesis engine." This exactly supports "convert text into phoneme codes and be adapted as a phonemizer/front end."

Part 2 (Kokoro link) is broadly correct but slightly overstated. The Kokoro repo (https://github.com/hexgrad/kokoro) lists espeak as a required dependency "used for English OOD fallback and some non-English languages." Kokoro's PRIMARY G2P is actually misaki, with espeak-ng as (a) the English out-of-distribution fallback and (b) the primary phonemizer for Spanish/French/Hindi/Italian/Portuguese. So calling it THE "phoneme backbone" for Kokoro with the word "exactly" mildly overstates its role versus misaki — but espeak-ng is genuinely a foundational, required phonemization dependency in Kokoro's pipeline.

The general principle is well-documented: StyleTTS2 (which Kokoro derives from) uses espeak's EspeakBackend phonemizer, and dedicated packages (phonemizer JOSS paper joss.03958, espeak-phonemizer on PyPI) exist precisely to use espeak-ng as the IPA front-end for neural TTS.

Verdict: The minor imprecision (espeak-ng is a fallback/secondary G2P in Kokoro, not the sole primary "backbone") is a qualification, not a contradiction. Core assertion is correct, current, and backed by primary sources matching the claim's strength.

### [15] Chatterbox is released under the MIT License, permitting commercial use and redistribution.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://github.com/resemble-ai/chatterbox (primary)
Quote: "MIT License — commercial use and redistribution permitted under MIT terms."
Verifier evidence (high): VERIFIED via primary source. The actual LICENSE file at github.com/resemble-ai/chatterbox/blob/master/LICENSE is the standard, unmodified MIT License text: opening line "MIT License", "Copyright (c) 2025 Resemble AI". The GitHub repo displays an "MIT license" badge. MIT terms inherently permit commercial use and redistribution, so the quote is an accurate (not overreaching) restatement. Corroborated by Resemble AI's official site (chatterbox-turbo page: "ships under the MIT license and can be used in personal, research, and commercial projects — including closed-source products") and Hugging Face (ResembleAI/chatterbox, 0.5B Llama backbone kept under MIT). The model variants (Chatterbox, Multilingual, Turbo) are all MIT. One caveat that does NOT refute the claim: all generated audio embeds Resemble's PerTh neural watermark; however Resemble explicitly states this imposes no usage restriction ("no restrictions on using a Chatterbox cloned voice, since this code is under the MIT license"). The watermark is an attribution feature, not a license term limiting commercial use or redistribution. No credible source disputes the MIT license or its commercial/redistribution permissions. Copyright dated 2025; current as of 2026.

### [16] Chatterbox supports Apple Silicon (MPS) and CPU inference in addition to CUDA, with device="mps" accepted in initialization, making it locally runnable on a Mac.
Vote: 2-1 · Source: https://github.com/resemble-ai/chatterbox (primary)
Quote: "GPU/CPU support: "cuda" or "cpu" / "mps" (Apple Silicon)—confirmed in code examples... MPS/Apple: Supported: `device="mps"` accepted in initialization."
Verifier evidence (high): SUPPORTED by primary source. Official resemble-ai/chatterbox README explicitly lists `device = "cuda"  # or "cpu" / "mps"` and the API `ChatterboxTTS.from_pretrained(device=...)` accepts "mps". The repo ships a dedicated example_for_mac.py using `device = "mps" if torch.backends.mps.is_available() else "cpu"`, confirming MPS is officially supported and device="mps" is accepted in initialization. Independent corroboration: devnen/Chatterbox-TTS-Server runs accelerated on Apple Silicon (MPS), plus community Apple Silicon ports (Jimmi42). The seemingly-contradicting Issue #357 ("doesn't work on Apple silicon due to lack of CUDA") is NOT a refutation — it is a specific, fixable torch.load map_location bug for one checkpoint (s3gen.pt), not a fundamental incompatibility; device="mps" initialization still works and the Mac example handles map_location. CAVEATS that qualify but do not refute: a known memory leak on Apple Silicon (Issue #218, ~222-800MB per few generations) and some CUDA-saved checkpoints needing map_location workarounds affect practicality/smoothness but not the truth of MPS acceptance. Claim is current (active commits/PRs through Dec 2025, e.g. PR #407 adding map_location for M1/MPS).

### [17] Chatterbox does not output word- or phoneme-level timestamps or alignment data, so it cannot natively produce a text-to-audio timing map.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://github.com/resemble-ai/chatterbox (primary)
Quote: "Word/Phoneme-Level Timestamps: Not mentioned. Documentation lacks alignment or timing data output."
Verifier evidence (high): Claim is well-supported by primary sources and current as of June 2026. The resemble-ai/chatterbox GitHub repo API returns audio only - model.generate returns a wav waveform with no timestamps, durations, or alignment. The Hugging Face model card ResembleAI/chatterbox confirms generate returns only wav, no timing metadata. Third-party servers (devnen, travisvn, petermg forks) are audio-only with no word/phoneme timestamp output. The only confound is the Resemble marketing phrase alignment-informed generation, but that describes an internal synthesis mechanism for latency/prosody, not exposed word/phoneme timestamps, so it does not contradict the claim. Getting timestamps from Chatterbox requires running an external forced aligner such as WhisperX or MFA on the generated wav, confirming Chatterbox does not natively emit alignment. The claim correctly states it cannot natively produce a text-to-audio timing map. Primary repo and model card source strength matches the claim strength.</parameter>
</invoke>


### [18] XTTS-v2 performs voice cloning from just a 6-second audio clip, enabling rapid addition of new voices for a multi-speaker (narrator + question) format.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://huggingface.co/coqui/XTTS-v2 (primary)
Quote: "Voice cloning with just a quick 6-second audio clip"
Verifier evidence (high): The HuggingFace XTTS-v2 model card (primary source, https://huggingface.co/coqui/XTTS-v2) features list states verbatim: "Voice cloning with just a 6-second audio clip" — an exact match to the supporting quote. Independently corroborated by multiple third-party sources (aimodels.fyi, promptquorum.com, localaimaster.com, semenov.work, BoltzmannEntropy/xtts2-ui) all confirming ~6s minimum reference audio for cloning. The official Coqui docs (docs.coqui.ai) even state a shorter "3-second audio clip," which reinforces rather than contradicts the short-clip cloning capability. Caveat (does not refute): 6s is a practical minimum; sources note 15-30s of clean audio yields a stronger voice match, and reference audio must be clean (no background noise/overlap). The "6-second" phrasing is vendor marketing language but is independently verified by real users. The inference that short-clip cloning enables rapid addition of narrator+question voices is sound. Note: model uses the restrictive Coqui Public Model License (CPML), relevant to the broader research but not to this specific cloning-duration claim.

### [19] XTTS-v2 is released under the Coqui Public Model License (CPML), a non-standard generative-model license whose commercial/redistribution terms are not stated on the model card and must be checked separately.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://huggingface.co/coqui/XTTS-v2 (primary)
Quote: "Coqui Public Model License"
Verifier evidence (high): VERIFIED against the primary source (HuggingFace coqui/XTTS-v2 model card). (1) License name: model card metadata shows "license: coqui-public-model-license"; README states "This model is licensed under Coqui Public Model License" (link coqui.ai/cpml). It is genuinely non-standard/bespoke for generative models — the README itself notes "There's a lot that goes into a license for generative models" and links an "origin story" blog (coqui.ai/blog/tts/cpml), not a standard OSI/CC license. (2) "Terms not stated on the model card": CONFIRMED — the entire license section of the README is just: "This model is licensed under [Coqui Public Model License](https://coqui.ai/cpml). There's a lot that goes into a license for generative models, and you can read more of the origin story of CPML here." No commercial or redistribution terms appear inline. (3) "Must be checked separately": CONFIRMED — terms live in external coqui.ai/cpml and LICENSE.txt. The claim is carefully scoped: it does NOT assert what the terms ARE (permissive vs restrictive), only that they are absent from the card and must be checked elsewhere — exactly what the primary source shows. Note: independent sources (aimodels.org, promptquorum, GitHub coqui-ai/TTS discussions #4145/#4304) actually disagree about whether CPML permits commercial use of outputs and report Coqui Inc shut down Jan 2024 with no one to sell a commercial license — this real-world ambiguity REINFORCES rather than refutes the claim that terms must be checked separately. No contradicting evidence found.

### [20] Zonos-v0.1 is released under the Apache-2.0 license, permitting commercial use and redistribution, which makes it license-compatible for an open/local educational-podcast pipeline.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://github.com/Zyphra/Zonos (primary)
Quote: "Apache-2.0 (permitting commercial use and redistribution)"
Verifier evidence (high): Claim is well-supported by multiple primary sources. (1) Zyphra's own official announcement (https://www.zyphra.com/our-work/beta-release-of-zonos-v0-1) states verbatim: "We are releasing our 1.6B transformer and 1.6B hybrid under an Apache 2.0 license" and "We release both of these models under the permissive Apache 2.0 license." (2) The primary GitHub repo (https://github.com/Zyphra/Zonos) lists "Apache-2.0 license" in its repo footer. (3) The HuggingFace model card (Zyphra/Zonos-v0.1-hybrid) corroborates. Apache-2.0 is a standard, well-understood permissive license that by its terms permits commercial use and redistribution (with attribution/NOTICE preservation), so the claim does not overreach in characterizing it. The claim is current and specific to v0.1 (a later Zonos-2 exists but the claim targets v0.1). CAVEAT (qualifies but does not refute the narrow license claim): GitHub Issue #45 ("Clarification on Commercial Use & Dataset License") raises a community concern that the ~200k hours of training data may include copyrighted material, so real-world commercial safety is debated — but this is a training-data-provenance question, NOT a contradiction of the license being Apache-2.0. The claim as worded ("released under Apache-2.0... permitting commercial use and redistribution... license-compatible") is accurate.

### [21] Zonos claims expressiveness and quality on par with or surpassing top commercial TTS providers, positioning it as a potential quality upgrade over Kokoro (though this is vendor self-description, not independent benchmark).
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://github.com/Zyphra/Zonos (primary)
Quote: "delivering expressiveness and quality on par with—or even surpassing—top TTS providers"
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is accurately supported by the primary source and is carefully hedged. The Zyphra/Zonos GitHub README states verbatim: "delivering expressiveness and quality on par with—or even surpassing—top TTS providers" (confirmed live via WebFetch and in the search result title). The claim faithfully paraphrases this AND explicitly flags it as "vendor self-description, not independent benchmark" — so it does not overclaim Zonos superiority as fact. Source quality matches claim strength: a vendor's own repo is the correct primary source for verifying what the vendor CLAIMS. Zyphra itself concedes the assessment is qualitative/subjective: "Given the challenges in quantitatively assessing audio quality, Zyphra believes that the generation quality produced by Zonos is on par with or even surpasses..." No credible source contradicts the framing. License (Apache-2.0) and model size (1.6B transformer/hybrid) are confirmed. Known limitations (audio artifacts, alignment issues per the README's own caveats) exist but do not refute a claim that only asserts what the vendor claims and labels it as marketing. The "(this is vendor self-description, not independent benchmark)" caveat is exactly what the research question asked for.

### [22] Zonos supports zero-shot voice cloning from a short reference sample plus fine-grained prosody/emotion conditioning (speaking rate, pitch, max frequency, audio quality, and emotions such as happiness, anger, sadness, fear), which is relevant for multi-voice narrator+question formats.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://github.com/Zyphra/Zonos (primary)
Quote: "Zero-shot TTS with voice cloning: Input desired text and a 10-30s speaker sample ... fine-grained control of many aspects of the generated audio. These include speaking rate, pitch, maximum frequency, audio quality, and various emotions such as happiness, anger, sadness, and fear"
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is a near-verbatim paraphrase of the primary source. The official Zyphra/Zonos GitHub README and the Hugging Face Zyphra/Zonos-v0.1-hybrid model card (Apache 2.0) both state verbatim: "Zero-shot TTS with voice cloning: Input desired text and a 10-30s speaker sample to generate high quality TTS output" and "fine-grained control of many aspects of the generated audio. These include speaking rate, pitch, maximum frequency, audio quality, and various emotions such as happiness, anger, sadness, and fear." Every specific element of the claim (10-30s sample, the four named control parameters, the exact four emotions) maps directly to documented features. No checklist item triggers refutation: (1) supported by quote, not overreach; (2) no credible source contradicts feature existence — independent commentary (testingcatalog.com, nolist.ai) corroborates the emotion sliders and voice cloning, with caveats only about output quality (occasional artifacts/alignment issues), which do not refute that the features exist; (3) source quality is sufficient — this is a feature-existence claim backed by the primary open-weight repo and HF card, not an extraordinary performance claim; (4) not outdated — Zonos v0.1 (Feb 2025) features as documented remain accurate even though a newer ZONOS2 exists; (5) not marketing fluff — the conditioning parameters are a documented, configurable API in an open-weight model, not a subjective quality boast. The "relevant for narrator+question formats" clause is a reasonable application inference (distinct voices via cloning + per-voice prosody control), not a refutable factual error.


## Refuted claims (for transparency)
- "Phone-level (phoneme) alignment is only directly available from MFA; end-to-end ASR aligners like MMS and WhisperX do not natively provide phone-level representations." (https://arxiv.org/html/2406.19363v1, vote 1-2)
- "XTTS-v2 supports 17 languages including American and British English, and allows multiple speaker references and interpolation between speakers (multi-speaker support)." (https://huggingface.co/coqui/XTTS-v2, vote 0-3)

## Instructions
1. Identify claims that say the same thing — merge them, combine their sources.
2. Group related claims into coherent findings. Each finding should directly address the research question.
3. Assign confidence per finding: high (multiple primary sources, unanimous votes), medium (secondary sources or split votes), low (single source or blog-quality).
4. Write a 3-5 sentence executive summary answering the research question.
5. Note caveats: what's uncertain, what sources were weak, what time-sensitivity applies.
6. List 2-4 open questions that emerged but weren't answered.

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- agents-sdk: Build AI agents on Cloudflare Workers using the Agents SDK. Load when creating stateful agents, durable workflows, real-time WebSocket apps, scheduled tasks, MCP servers, chat applications, voice agents, or browser automation. Covers Agent class, state management, callable RPC, Workflows, durable execution, queues, retries, observability, and React hooks. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- cloudflare: Comprehensive Cloudflare platform skill covering Workers, Pages, storage (KV, D1, R2), AI (Workers AI, Vectorize, Agents SDK), feature flags (Flagship), networking (Tunnel, Spectrum), security (WAF, DDoS), and infrastructure-as-code (Terraform, Pulumi). Use for any Cloudflare development task. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- cloudflare-email-service: Send and receive transactional emails with Cloudflare Email Service (Email Sending + Email Routing). Use when building email sending (Workers binding or REST API), email routing, Agents SDK email handling, or integrating email into any app — Workers, Node.js, Python, Go, etc. Also use for email deliverability, SPF/DKIM/DMARC, wrangler email setup, MCP email tools, or when a coding agent needs to send emails. Even for simple requests like "add email to my Worker" — this skill has critical config details.
- cloudflare-one: Guides Cloudflare One Zero Trust and SASE work across Access, Gateway, WARP, Tunnel, Cloudflare WAN, DLP, CASB, device posture, and identity. Use when designing, configuring, troubleshooting, or reviewing Cloudflare One deployments. Retrieval-first: use current Cloudflare docs/API schemas instead of embedded product docs.
- cloudflare-one-migrations: Plans migrations from Zscaler ZIA/ZPA, Palo Alto, legacy VPN, SWG, or SASE stacks to Cloudflare One. Use for migration assessments, policy mapping, rollout plans, and parity/gap analysis.
- durable-objects: Create and review Cloudflare Durable Objects. Use when building stateful coordination (chat rooms, multiplayer games, booking systems), implementing RPC methods, SQLite storage, alarms, WebSockets, or reviewing DO code for best practices. Covers Workers integration, wrangler config, and testing with Vitest. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- sandbox-sdk: Build sandboxed applications for secure code execution. Load when building AI code execution, code interpreters, CI/CD systems, interactive dev environments, or executing untrusted code. Covers Sandbox SDK lifecycle, commands, files, code interpreter, and preview URLs. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- turnstile-spin: Set up Cloudflare Turnstile end-to-end in a project — scan the codebase, create the widget via the Cloudflare API, deploy the managed siteverify Worker, write the frontend snippets, validate, and persist the skill. Load this when a user asks to add Turnstile, set up CAPTCHA, protect a form from bots, or fix a Turnstile integration. Mirrors developers.cloudflare.com/turnstile/spin.
- web-perf: Analyzes web performance using Chrome DevTools MCP. Measures Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS) and supplementary metrics (FCP, TBT, Speed Index), identifies render-blocking resources, network dependency chains, layout shifts, caching issues, and accessibility gaps. Use when asked to audit, profile, debug, or optimize page load performance, Lighthouse scores, or site speed. Biases towards retrieval from current documentation over pre-trained knowledge.
- workers-best-practices: Reviews and authors Cloudflare Workers code against production best practices. Load when writing new Workers, reviewing Worker code, configuring wrangler.jsonc, or checking for common Workers anti-patterns (streaming, floating promises, global state, secrets, bindings, observability). Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- wrangler: Cloudflare Workers CLI for deploying, developing, and managing Workers, KV, R2, D1, Vectorize, Hyperdrive, Workers AI, Containers, Queues, Workflows, Pipelines, and Secrets Store. Load before running wrangler commands to ensure correct syntax and best practices. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll synthesize the verified claims into a structured research report. The research has already been completed and verified—my task is to merge duplicates, group into findings, and synthesize.

input.summary: For an English educational-podcast pipeline currently on Kokoro-82M, the verified evidence does NOT identify a slam-dunk open/local model that beats Kokoro on independently-measured naturalness — the strongest quality claims for candidates (Zonos, XTTS-v2, Chatterbox) are either vendor self-description or feature-existence facts, and the right way to rank naturalness is blind pairwise human preference (TTS Arena's Elo-style leaderboard) rather than MOS or WER, which are noted as unreliable/small-scale. The most defensible upgrades are operational rather than model swaps: re-host Kokoro itself on Apple's MLX via the MIT-licensed mlx-audio (54 voice presets, native Apple Silicon optimization), and add a separate forced-alignment stage to get the word/phoneme timing map Kokoro and Chatterbox do not natively emit. For alignment, the peer-reviewed benchmark evidence favors Montreal Forced Aligner (MFA) over WhisperX and MMS for English word-level accuracy (89.4% vs 82.4% vs 75.7% at 50ms on TIMIT), while ctc-forced-aligner and mlx-audio's Qwen3-ForcedAligner/Parakeet offer convenient JSON/word-timestamp outputs on a Mac. For the high-speed/ultra track, foundational psychoacoustics (Mach1) shows nonuniform time-compression stays comprehensible to ~2.5-4.2x real time and beats linear compression by ~5-31 points, and eSpeak NG / DECtalk provide the crisp formant "robotic" option — though DECtalk's proprietary FONIX license is a redistribution red flag whereas eSpeak NG is safe. On licensing, Chatterbox (MIT) and Zonos-v0.1 (Apache-2.0) are redistribution-friendly, while XTTS-v2's CPML terms are unstated on the model card and must be checked separately (Coqui is defunct, adding risk).

findings.claim: Naturalness should be ranked by blind pairwise human preference (TTS Arena), not MOS/WER; TTS Arena uses an Elo-style leaderboard with model identities hidden until after voting.
findings.evidence: HF blog states users 'vote on which model sounds more natural' with names 'revealed only after a vote is submitted,' ranked by 'an algorithm similar to the Elo rating system, commonly used in chess.' The blog explicitly motivates this by noting objective WER is 'unreliable' and MOS studies are 'typically small-scale.' This is the authoritative methodology for comparing candidate TTS naturalness; note that none of the verified claims supply an actual head-to-head Arena ranking of Kokoro vs candidates, so concrete naturalness-vs-Kokoro deltas remain unestablished in this evidence set.

findings.claim: No verified candidate is shown by an independent benchmark to beat Kokoro on naturalness; the strongest quality claims (Zonos 'on par with or surpassing top providers') are explicit vendor self-description, not independent evaluation.
findings.evidence: The Zonos README claims 'expressiveness and quality on par with—or even surpassing—top TTS providers,' and the verified claim itself flags this as vendor marketing, with Zyphra conceding the assessment is 'qualitative/subjective.' Other candidate claims (XTTS-v2, Chatterbox) are feature-existence facts (cloning duration, license, device support), not naturalness comparisons. Verdict: quality-vs-Kokoro is not independently established in this evidence set — switching on quality grounds alone is not yet justified.

findings.claim: Kokoro can be re-hosted on Apple Silicon via the MIT-licensed mlx-audio (Apple MLX framework), which natively supports Kokoro with 54 voice presets including the pipeline's existing voices.
findings.evidence: mlx-audio (MIT, Prince Canuma/Blaizzy) is 'built on Apple's MLX framework' with 'Fast inference optimized for Apple Silicon (M series chips)' and loads Kokoro via mlx-community quantizations (Kokoro-82M-bf16/8bit/4bit) with 54 voice presets including af_heart, bm_george etc. This is the lowest-effort, lowest-risk upgrade: keep Kokoro's voices and two-voice format, gain M-series acceleration over CPU/MPS, MIT license is redistribution-safe. Verdict: worth adopting as a drop-in re-host.

findings.claim: Kokoro (and Chatterbox) do not natively emit word/phoneme timestamps; a forced-alignment stage is required to build a text-to-audio timing map for speed-compression, highlighting, and script mapping.
findings.evidence: Chatterbox's generate() returns only a wav with 'no timestamps, durations, or alignment'; the verified claim notes getting timing requires running WhisperX or MFA on the output. Kokoro likewise has no native alignment in this evidence set. Implication: alignment must be added as a separate stage regardless of which synthesizer is chosen.

findings.claim: For English word-level forced alignment, Montreal Forced Aligner (MFA, GMM-HMM/Kaldi) outperforms modern ASR-based aligners WhisperX and MMS — 89.4% vs 82.4% vs 75.7% accuracy at 50ms tolerance on TIMIT.
findings.evidence: Peer-reviewed Interspeech 2024 paper (Rousso et al.) reports MFA outperforming WhisperX and MMS at ALL tolerance thresholds; at 50ms on TIMIT, MFA 89.4% / WhisperX 82.4% / MMS 75.7%, and at 10ms 41.6% / 22.4% / 18.6%. Independent practitioner corroboration (WhisperX issue #1247) confirms WhisperX timestamps drift vs MFA. Caveat: the comparison only scores words the ASR systems correctly recognized, on English read/conversational speech, using ASR not purpose-built for FA. Verdict: MFA is the accuracy-best aligner for the timing map; WhisperX/MMS are convenient but less precise.

findings.claim: ctc-forced-aligner produces a text-to-audio timing map as JSON (start/end/text) from existing audio using HuggingFace CTC models (Wav2Vec2/HuBERT/MMS), with configurable word/char/sentence granularity and SRT/WebVTT export.
findings.evidence: README documents forced alignment 'between text and audio' via Wav2Vec2/HuBERT/MMS, default model mms-300m-1130-forced-aligner, JSON output with start/end/text per segment, plus generate_srt()/generate_webvtt() and split_size granularity control. Verdict: a practical, easy-to-integrate aligner; trades MFA's accuracy edge for simpler Python integration.

findings.claim: On a Mac, mlx-audio provides native alignment/timestamps via Qwen3-ForcedAligner (word/char-level start/end times) and Parakeet (sentence/word-level timestamps), enabling an end-to-end Apple Silicon timing-map workflow.
findings.evidence: mlx-audio loads Qwen3-ForcedAligner-0.6B emitting per-item start_time/end_time/text; the official Qwen card confirms 'word or character level timestamps' for 'arbitrary units within up to 5 minutes of speech in 11 languages' (reports cite ~6ms MAE). Parakeet docs show 'Sentence and Word Timestamps' (AlignedSentence/AlignedToken). Verdict: lets both synthesis (Kokoro) and alignment run on MLX/Apple Silicon in one stack, though MFA still wins on raw accuracy.

findings.claim: Nonuniform (Mach1) time-scale modification keeps speech comprehensible up to ~2.5-4.2x real time (390-673 wpm) and beats linear compression by ~5-31 percentage points (about 17 pp average in that range), with ~15% faster response times and no significant comprehension loss as rate increases across that range.
findings.evidence: Peer-reviewed Covell/Withgott/Slaney Mach1 paper (IEEE ICASSP 1998): comprehensible to 2.56-4.15x real time; nonuniform beats linear by 5-31 pp (content-dependent, ~17 pp average), response times -15%, no significant loss across 2.5-4.2x. Caveats: single n=14 inventor-run study; later work attributes some benefit to silence/pause removal and finds linear can win for speech-in-noise/word-intelligibility — but those are out-of-scope of the comprehension-at-2.5-4.2x finding. Verdict: supports a content-aware (nonuniform) speed-compression track over naive linear speedup; a forced-alignment timing map is what enables content-aware compression.

findings.claim: For an extreme high-speed/'ultra' robotic track, eSpeak NG is the safe formant choice — clear and usable at high speeds (less natural than neural), redistribution-friendly, and doubles as a phonemizer/G2P front end (as used by Kokoro/StyleTTS2).
findings.evidence: espeak-ng README: formant synthesis, 'clear, and can be used at high speeds, but is not as natural...than larger synthesizers based on human speech recordings,' and 'can translate text into phoneme codes...adapted as a front end for another speech synthesis engine.' Used in NVDA/screen readers (a high-WPM use case). Qualification: in Kokoro, misaki is the primary G2P and espeak-ng is the OOD/non-English fallback, so calling it THE backbone slightly overstates its role. Verdict: ideal crisp formant option for the 16x ultra track; license is permissive (safe to redistribute).

findings.claim: DECtalk is a locally-buildable formant (Klatt-based) synthesizer for the robotic/ultra track on macOS (Apple Silicon + Intel), but its proprietary FONIX license makes it a redistribution red flag.
findings.evidence: github.com/dectalk/dectalk builds for 'MacOS/iOS (Apple Silicon and Intel)' plus Linux/Windows/WebAssembly (MacPorts port confirms macOS packaging independently). However the LICENCE is proprietary FONIX: 'use, or copying...authorized only pursuant to a valid written license from FONIX,' and IP owners are defunct. Verdict: technically feasible and distinctive 'voice of Stephen Hawking' formant sound, but do NOT use in a redistributed pipeline without authorization — prefer eSpeak NG for the robotic track if redistribution matters.

findings.claim: Chatterbox (Resemble AI) is MIT-licensed (commercial use/redistribution OK) and runs locally on a Mac via device='mps' (and CPU), but emits no alignment and embeds a non-restrictive PerTh watermark.
findings.evidence: LICENSE is unmodified MIT (Copyright 2025 Resemble AI); Resemble confirms commercial/closed-source use OK. README lists device='mps' (Apple Silicon) and ships example_for_mac.py. Caveats: known Apple Silicon memory leak (issue #218) and some CUDA-saved checkpoints need map_location workarounds (PR #407); all output carries a PerTh watermark (attribution, not a usage restriction); no native timestamps. Verdict: license-clean, Mac-runnable candidate worth A/B-testing alongside Kokoro on naturalness, but practicality friction (memory leak) and no alignment mean it does not displace the MLX+aligner approach by itself.

findings.claim: XTTS-v2 (Coqui) offers fast 6-second voice cloning (good for rapidly adding narrator+question voices), but its Coqui Public Model License (CPML) terms are not on the model card and must be checked separately — and Coqui is defunct, leaving commercial terms ambiguous.
findings.evidence: Model card: 'Voice cloning with just a 6-second audio clip' (docs even cite 3s; 15-30s clean audio yields better match). License is 'coqui-public-model-license' with NO commercial/redistribution terms stated inline — they live at coqui.ai/cpml. Independent sources disagree on whether CPML permits commercial output use, and Coqui Inc shut down Jan 2024 with no one to sell a commercial license. Note a refuted sub-claim: XTTS-v2's specific '17 languages / multi-speaker interpolation' assertion was voted down (0-3), so do not rely on that. Verdict: strong cloning ergonomics but a licensing risk for a redistributed pipeline — proceed only after resolving CPML.

findings.claim: Zonos-v0.1 is Apache-2.0 (commercial/redistribution OK) and supports zero-shot cloning from a 10-30s sample plus fine-grained prosody/emotion control (rate, pitch, max frequency, audio quality; happiness/anger/sadness/fear), useful for a multi-voice narrator+question format.
findings.evidence: Zyphra releases the 1.6B transformer and hybrid under Apache-2.0 (permits commercial use/redistribution). README documents zero-shot cloning from a 10-30s sample and fine-grained control of speaking rate, pitch, max frequency, audio quality, and emotions. Caveats: README itself notes audio artifacts/alignment issues; quality-vs-Kokoro is vendor self-described, not benchmarked; issue #45 raises training-data provenance concerns (does not affect the Apache-2.0 license itself). Verdict: the most license-friendly higher-control candidate to A/B-test for distinct expressive narrator/question voices, but validate quality and artifact behavior on your own content before switching.

input.caveats: 1) No verified claim provides an independent, head-to-head naturalness/MOS or TTS-Arena ranking of Kokoro versus the candidate models. Quality-superiority statements for Zonos (and implicitly XTTS-v2/Chatterbox) are vendor self-description or feature facts — the actual 'beats Kokoro on naturalness' question is UNRESOLVED in this evidence set and would need fresh blind A/B testing on your own scripts. 2) Several practicality numbers (RTF/batch-render speed, RAM/VRAM, latency) requested in the question are not covered by any verified claim; only directional facts (mlx-audio is Apple-Silicon-optimized; Chatterbox has an MPS memory leak) survived. 3) The MFA>WhisperX>MMS alignment result is scoped to English read/conversational corpora (TIMIT/Buckeye) and only scores correctly-recognized words; it may not transfer identically to synthesized podcast audio. 4) Mach1 comprehension figures are foundational (1998), from a single n=14 inventor-run study, and the broader 'nonuniform always wins' narrative is contested for speech-in-noise and word-intelligibility — they hold specifically for comprehension at 2.5-4.2x. 5) Licensing is time-sensitive and provider-dependent: DECtalk (proprietary FONIX) and XTTS-v2 (CPML, Coqui defunct) are redistribution red flags; Chatterbox (MIT), Zonos-v0.1 (Apache-2.0), mlx-audio (MIT), and eSpeak NG are the redistribution-safe options. 6) One sub-claim about XTTS-v2 languages/multi-speaker interpolation and one about MFA-only phone-level alignment were REFUTED — do not rely on them. 7) Kokoro's own native-alignment status is inferred (no positive claim that it lacks alignment was independently verified the way Chatterbox's was), though the practical conclusion — add a separate aligner — is robust either way.
openQuestions: What does a blind A/B / TTS-Arena-style test on your own educational-podcast scripts actually show for naturalness and high-speed intelligibility of Kokoro vs Zonos-v0.1, Chatterbox, XTTS-v2, and StyleTTS2/F5-TTS/Fish-Speech (the latter three were named in the question but produced no surviving verified claims)?

What are the concrete local-inference numbers on Apple Silicon (real-time factor, batch render time, RAM, MPS/CoreML/ONNX support, latency) for each candidate versus Kokoro-on-MLX — none were captured in the verified evidence?

Does MFA's accuracy advantage over WhisperX/ctc-forced-aligner hold on synthesized (TTS-generated) podcast audio specifically, and which aligner gives the best precision-vs-integration-effort tradeoff for content-aware speed compression?

What are the exact CPML terms for XTTS-v2 commercial/redistribution use now that Coqui is defunct, and is there any pathway to a redistribution-safe license — i.e., can XTTS-v2 be used at all in a published pipeline?

content.text: I've synthesized the 23 verified claims into 13 coherent findings, merging semantic duplicates (e.g., the three Mach1 claims into one, the three mlx-audio claims into one, the two MFA-accuracy claims into one, and the two each for DECtalk, XTTS-v2, and Zonos). The structured report is complete.
